questions.question: Explain the Test-and-Set (TSL) instruction and how it provides mutual exclusion in a uniprocessor system.
keyPoints: TSL atomically reads a memory location and writes a non-zero value to it in a single instruction
The old value is returned; if it was 0, the process acquires the lock
If returned value is non-zero (1), the lock is already held and process retries
This solves the race condition in entering critical section

questions.question: What are the limitations of software-based synchronization algorithms like Peterson's solution in modern multiprocessor systems?
keyPoints: Assumes atomic memory reads and writes which hardware does not guarantee
Assumes sequential memory access ordering which modern processors violate
Memory consistency models allow reorderings across processors
Cache coherence issues cause stale value reads on multiprocessors

questions.question: Describe the Compare-and-Swap (CAS) instruction and explain how it can be used to implement lock-free synchronization.
keyPoints: CAS compares memory location's current value with expected value
If match, atomically updates to new value and returns success
If no match, returns failure without modifying
Retry loop allows building atomic operations like increment, push, etc.

questions.question: What is busy-waiting (spinlock)? Discuss its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Spinlock: process continuously checks lock variable in a loop while waiting
Advantages: simple to implement, no context switch overhead, good for short waits
Disadvantages: wastes CPU cycles, inefficient on uniprocessors, can cause priority inversion
Better for multi-core systems with short critical sections

questions.question: Explain Load-Linked and Store-Conditional operations. How do they work together to provide atomic operations?
keyPoints: Load-Linked reads value and marks (links) the memory location
Store-Conditional succeeds only if location hasn't been modified since last LL
If successful, write completes atomically; if fails, must retry entire sequence
Enables atomic read-modify-write without lock overhead

questions.question: What is a memory barrier (fence) instruction? Why is it necessary in modern multi-core systems?
keyPoints: Memory barrier forces all pending memory operations to complete before proceeding
Ensures memory ordering consistency between different processor cores
Prevents instruction reordering that could cause race conditions
Different types: full fence, store fence, load fence

questions.question: Explain the Swap instruction algorithm for mutual exclusion. How does it differ from TSL-based approach?
keyPoints: Swap atomically exchanges lock variable with a register containing a value
Process sets register to 1, swaps with lock (containing 0), gets old lock value
If old value was 0, lock acquired; otherwise retry
Unlike TSL: always writes new value, no need to reset lock explicitly

questions.question: Compare and contrast Test-and-Set (TSL) and Compare-and-Swap (CAS) synchronization primitives. Discuss their relative advantages and typical use cases.
keyPoints: TSL: unconditional write, always returns old value; CAS: conditional write, may fail
TSL simpler but can cause more contention; CAS more flexible for lock-free algorithms
CAS solves ABA problem that TSL cannot handle directly
TSL better for simple spinlocks; CAS better for complex atomic data structures
Both require hardware support; CAS more portable across architectures
Performance: CAS may retry multiple times under high contention

questions.question: Explain the critical section problem and discuss how hardware-based synchronization solutions address the mutual exclusion requirement. What are the trade-offs involved?
keyPoints: Critical section: code segment accessing shared resources requiring mutual exclusion
Hardware solutions: atomic instructions (TSL, Swap, CAS, LL/SC)
Advantages: work on multiprocessors, correct with memory consistency models, efficient for short waits
Disadvantages: busy-waiting wastes CPU, potential deadlock if not careful, no blocking/waking
Trade-off: simplicity and correctness vs CPU efficiency and blocking capability
Software solutions (semaphores) built on hardware primitives provide better abstraction

questions.question: Discuss the ABA problem in synchronization. How does it occur and what mechanisms (like CAS) help address it?
keyPoints: ABA: value changes from A to B and back to A between check and update
Can cause lost updates in lock-free data structures
Example: stack push operation reads top as A, another thread pops A and pushes B, original thread sees A again and incorrectly thinks stack unchanged
CAS alone doesn't prevent ABA - only detects if value is still expected
Solutions: tagged pointers (add version counter), double-width CAS (CAS2)
Hardware support like Load-Linked/Store-Conditional can detect intervening modifications

questions.question: Design and explain a spinlock implementation using the Test-and-Set instruction. Also discuss scenarios where spinlocks are appropriate versus inappropriate.
keyPoints: Implementation: while (TSL(lock) != 0); // busy wait; critical section; lock = 0;
Appropriate when: critical section is very short, lock held briefly, running on multi-core, blocking would cost more than spinning
Inappropriate when: critical section long (wastes CPU), single-core (no benefit), high contention (wasteful), real-time systems (unpredictable)
Improvements: adaptive spinlocks (spin briefly then block), exponential backoff, pause instructions
